segments.s432-g153: همانست is the demonstrative 'همان' + copula 'ست' (it is the same/it is precisely so). کز is the archaic/poetic relative 'که' (here 'that'/'which'), introducing the clause that follows. گز modifies بهانه (poetic word-order and omission of a linking ezafe is common), so گز بهانه منم reads 'I am the pretext of the gaz/tamarisk' with منم ('من' + enclitic copula 'ـم') meaning 'I am.'
segments.s432-g154: وزین here functions as an adjective ('weighty/heavy' or 'grave') modifying تیرگی ('darkness'). در is the preposition 'in', and فسانه is the noun 'fable/legend' (object of در). The final منم again is the first-person copula 'I am'; the clause therefore says 'I am the heavy darkness in the tale.' The doubled 'منم' in the two clauses is a rhetorical repetition for emphasis.
segments.s432-g129: بدین چوب شد روزگارم به سر is an idiomatic, slightly archaic phrasing: بدین چوب = 'with/by this stick' (بدین is an older form meaning 'by/with this'); شد is the past of شدن 'to become/come to be' and here marks the state/change; روزگارم ('my time/fate') is the subject/affected thing; به سر (with شدن) forms the idiom به سر شدن = 'to come to an end'. Word order is topical: instrument first, then the verbal phrase and the experiencer.
segments.s432-g130: ز سیمرغ وز رستم چاره‌گر attaches a cause/agent phrase to the previous clause: ز = 'from/because of'; سیمرغ is the mythical bird (proper noun); وز here is a fused form representing و + ز ('and from'), so the meaning is 'and from'; رستم چاره‌گر names Rostam with the epithet چاره‌گر 'the solver' or 'resourceful one'. The phrase names the sources/agents of the misfortune.
segments.s432-g131: فسونها و نیرنگها زال ساخت is a straightforward SOV phrase with plural objects first: فسونها 'enchantments' and نیرنگها 'tricks/stratagems' are the direct objects; زال is the agent/subject; ساخت is the past of ساختن 'made/devised'. The coordination is plain (فسونها و نیرنگها).
segments.s432-g132: که اروند و بند جهان او شناخت is a relative/result clause introduced by که ('that/so that/who'): اروند (the Arvand river) and بند جهان ('the bond(s) of the world') are the objects, او is the subject 'he', and شناخت is the verb 'knew/recognized/learned' (here used in the past sense). The clause explains the effect/purpose of Zal's actions.
segments.s432-g133: چو اسفندیار این سخن یاد کرد is a temporal clause: چو = 'when'; اسفندیار is the subject (proper name); این سخن = 'this speech/these words' is the object; یاد کرد is the compound verb 'to remember/recall' formed by the noun یاد + auxiliary/verb کردن, here in past tense کرد 'he remembered/recalled'.
segments.s432-g134: بپیچید و بگریست رستم به درد contains two coordinated verbs describing Rostam's reaction: بپیچید is the past form of پیچیدن with the prefix 'ب' (here 'he twisted/turned/writhed'); و = 'and'; بگریست is past of گریستن 'he wept/laments'; رستم names the experiencer; به درد = 'with pain/in sorrow' (prepositional phrase marking manner/condition).
segments.s432-g135: چنین گفت کز دیو ناسازگار begins the reported speech: چنین گفت = 'he said thus' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = 'said'); کز is a fused form of که + ز meaning 'that/from which' introducing the content clause; دیو ناسازگار = 'a hostile/inimical demon' (دیو = demon, ناسازگار = unfriendly or incompatible).
segments.s432-g136: ترا بهره رنج من آمد به کار is an idiomatic result clause: ترا (ت + را written separately here) = 'to you / you (object)' with را the object marker; بهره = 'share/portion'; رنج من = 'my suffering' (رنج = suffering, من = my); آمد به کار = a verbal phrase 'came into use / took effect' (آمد + به کار). The whole means 'your share of my suffering came into effect (because of that demon)'.
segments.s432-g137: چنانست (literary/poetic copular phrase) = “such is (he)”; کو here functions as a relative/archaic 'who/that' (not the interrogative 'where') introducing the relative clause; گفت is the verb 'to say/speak' (here used in the sense 'speaks') placed before its object (typical older Persian order); یکسر modifies سخن ('utterly/straightforwardly a single speech') and comes before the noun it quantifies.
segments.s432-g138: This clause completes the relative: ز مردی = 'against/from a man' (classical z often marks the object or source); به کژی is a prepositional phrase 'to/with crookedness/wrong' describing the content of the slur; نیفگند is the negative perfect/presentative form 'does not cast/throw' (archaic negative prefix نی- + افگند), and بن is an old/poetic element whose precise object sense is not directly represented by the supplied senses (here functioning as an enclitic/old object or emphatic particle attached to the verb phrase).
segments.s432-g139: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause ('for/that/as'); تا here means 'as long as/until' marking duration; من is the subject 'I'; به گیتی uses به + noun in an idiomatic locative/experiential sense 'in/throughout the world'; کمر بسته‌ام is a perfect construction 'I have girded my loins' (کمر + بستن in first person present perfect).
segments.s432-g140: بسی here is an adverb meaning 'many/much' (poetic 'bisy'), modifying the noun رزم 'battles'; گردنکشان is a plural/collective noun 'those who stretch/throw their necks' i.e. rebels/proud ones; جسته‌ام is the perfect of جستن 'I have sought/undertaken' — together 'I have undertaken many battles with/against the proud/rebels.'
segments.s432-g141: سواری is a noun 'rider/horseman'; ندیدم is the past negative/perfect 'I did not see / I have not seen'; چو here functions as 'like/as' making a simile; اسفندیار is the proper name in the object of the comparison. Word order places the negated verb before the comparative marker (typical in classical style).
segments.s432-g142: زره‌دار is an adjectival/noun compound 'armored one' (lit. 'bearer of armor') describing the rider; با introduces accompaniment/instrument 'with'; جوشن is a specific type of armor/ cuirass; کارزار is 'battle/field' indicating purpose — 'armored with a cuirass for battle.'
segments.s432-g143: چو = 'when' (temporal); بیچاره is a predicate/adjective 'miserable/unfortunate' applied to the speaker; برگشتم is the past tense 'I turned/returned'; از marks the source 'from'; دست اوی = 'his hand/strength' (اوی is an archaic/heavy-form pronoun meaning 'his/her'). The clause describes the moment of turning away from his grip/power.
segments.s432-g144: بدیدم is the past 'I saw'; the following noun series lists instruments/limbs: کمان 'bow'; و is the coordinating 'and'; بر here is a short lexeme that in this context names a weapon (supply of senses did not include that meaning), so its supplied senses don't match (the word functions as 'blade/sword/weapon'); the next و again coordinates; شست اوی literally 'his thumb/hand' (شست = thumb/hand) with اوی marking possession — the list enumerates what the speaker saw belonging to the opponent.
segments.s432-g1: بدانست رستم که = 'Rostam knew/realized that'. بدَانَست is a past-tense narrative verb 'he knew/realized' placed before the subject (رستم) for a slightly emphatic/poetic word order; که introduces the subordinate clause that follows.
segments.s432-g2: لابه به کار نیاید همی پیش اسفندیار = 'that pleading (or entreaty) will not be of use before Esfandiyar.' نیاید is present/subjunctive/negative sense 'does not come/does not avail' used here idiomatically; همی is a narrative/emphatic particle (here 'just/indeed' or continuous flavor) and پیش + name means 'in the presence of / before Esfandiyar.' The noun phrase لابه به کار functions as the subject/thing that 'does not avail.'
segments.s432-g3: کمان را به زه کرد = literally 'made the bow to the string' i.e. 'he drew the bowstring to the bow.' را is the definite/object marker attached to کمان, به + زه indicates the relation 'to the string,' and کرد is the simple past verb 'did/made' used here in the sense 'drew/strung' (poetic, slightly compressed phrasing).
segments.s432-g4: که پیکانش را داده بد آب رز = 'which had its arrow/shaft given (treated) with bad/ill rose-water.' که introduces the relative clause. پیکانش (پیکان + enclitic ש) = 'its/that arrow'; را marks the object of داده ('given/applied'); داده is a perfect participial form 'having given' or 'was given'; بد آب رز is a compound where بد qualifies the following آب (water) of رز (rose); the whole phrase describes a treatment of the arrow.
segments.s432-g5: همی راند تیر گز اندر کمان = 'he kept driving/pushing the tamarisk-arrow into the bow.' همی gives a continuous/habitual sense ('kept / continuously'), راند is a past tense verb 'drove/forced/pushed,' تیر گز is the type of arrow (گز = tamarisk), and اندر کمان = 'into/within the bow.' This is compressed narrative word order typical of Ferdowsi.
segments.s432-g6: سر خویش کرده سوی آسمان = 'having made/turned its head toward the sky.' سر = 'head/point' (here of the arrow); خویش + ش (written as separate items here) functions as the reflexive/enclitic 'its/own' (the split is orthographic in the annotation); کرده is a perfect participle meaning 'having made/turned,' followed by سوی آسمان 'toward the sky.'
segments.s432-g7: همی گفت کای پاک دادار هور = 'Then he said: O pure Creator, Hoor...' همی is the narrative particle marking the action; گفت here introduces speech ('said') though the annotated senses list is non-standard; کای is a vocative 'O,' پاک دادار is a compound epithet 'pure Creator,' and هور is an epithet/name addressed alongside the Creator (poetic vocative).
segments.s432-g8: فزاینده دانش و فر و زور = a series of epithets: 'increaser/augmenter of knowledge, of royal glory (فر), and of strength (زور).' فزاینده is a present-participial adjective 'increasing/augmenting' that governs the following nouns linked by و, forming a laudatory address.
segments.s432-g25: Subject (Tahmatan) comes first; گز (a measure, 'a gaz') is placed before the locative اندر کمان ('into the bow'), forming the amount by which the bow was drawn. راند is a poetic/past verb 'drove/drew' used transitively here (he drew the bow by that span). زود is an adverb 'quickly' that follows the verb in this poetic word order.
segments.s432-g26: بران سان = 'in that way / thus' (بران = 'on/that', سان = 'manner/like'); که introduces a relative clause. سیمرغ فرموده بود is a reported/past perfect construction: فرموده (honorific/past participle) + بود as an auxiliary gives 'had commanded/ordered'. The whole segment qualifies how he drew the bow.
segments.s432-g27: بزد is a simple past verb 'he struck/shot'; تیر is the direct object 'arrow'; بر + چشم marks the target 'against/at the eye' (poetic use of بر for 'at/against'); اسفندیار is the proper name of the injured man placed after the target.
segments.s432-g28: Literal order is adjective (سیه) + شد (became) + subject (جهان) — 'the world became dark.' پیش functions as 'before / in the presence of' and آن نامدار ('that renowned one') is the phrase indicating in whose presence the world darkened. شد is the past of 'to become'.
segments.s432-g29: خم آورد is a causative phrase: خم (bend) with آورد ('caused/brought about') = 'caused to bend' or 'bent'. بالای is a prepositional phrase 'upon the top of', سرو is 'cypress' and سهی is a poetic epithet placed after the noun as an adjective.
segments.s432-g30: ازو دور شد is an idiomatic verb phrase 'became distant from him' (= 'departed from him'); شد is the past of 'become'. دانش و فرهی are the compound subjects 'knowledge and refinement/nobility' that are said to have left him.
segments.s432-g31: نگون شد is a passive/state expression 'became overturned/overthrown'; سر is the grammatical subject 'head' placed after the verb in this poetic order; شاه یزدان‌پرست is a genitive phrase 'the God-worshipping king' specifying whose head it was.
segments.s432-g32: بیفتاد is past 'fell'; چاچی here is a vocative (address to the nurse/a bystander) interjected in the middle of the line. کمانش is 'his bow' (کمان + possessive -ش). ز is the poetic form of 'from', and دست is 'hand' — together 'the bow fell from his hand'.
segments.s432-g49: سر تیر is a genitive-like noun phrase 'the head/tip of the arrow' placed before the verb as the object; بگرفت is a past tense verb (بـ- prefix + گرفت) meaning 'seized/took hold of'. و links the following verb phrase. بیرون کشید is a compound verbal idea 'pulled out' (بیرون = out; کشید = past of کشیدن 'to pull/draw'), with the object expressed before the verbs.
segments.s432-g50: همی is an adverb here ('just/then' in epic narrative) modifying the following action. پر و پیکانش is a coordinated object 'feather and his arrow' (possession marked on پیکان by -ش). در خون is a prepositional phrase 'in/through blood' and کشید is the past verb 'drew/dragged' — the verb takes the coordinated object and the blood-phrase as an adverbial complement.
segments.s432-g51: همانگه is a temporal adverb 'at that moment'. به بهمن is an indirect object 'to Bahman' (به + proper name). رسید is the past tense 'reached/arrived' and آگهی is the subject 'news/information' (word order is inverted for poetic effect: temporal phrase + indirect object + verb + subject).
segments.s432-g52: که introduces the content clause 'that ...'. تیره شد is a two-word predicate: تیره (adjective 'dark/ominous') + شد (past of شدن 'became'), so 'became dark'. آن فر شاهنشهی is the noun phrase 'that royal/sovereign farr (splendor)', with شاهنشهی qualifying فر; the order puts the predicate before the affected noun phrase in poetic style.
segments.s432-g53: بیامد is past tense 'came'. به پیش (پیش = 'before, in the presence of') marks the person to whom he came; پشوتن is the proper name Peshotan. بگفت is the past-reporting verb 'he said/told' (بـ- prefix + گفت). The two verbs are presented in sequence: 'came before X and said...'.
segments.s432-g54: که introduces reported content 'that ...'. پیکار ما is 'our battle/fight' (possessive ما). گشت is past of گشتن here meaning 'became/turned (into)'. با introduces the accompanying circumstance 'with'; درد جفت is a compact phrase meaning 'paired/twin pain(s)' — جفت used as 'pair' to mean 'twofold' or 'twin' suffering. Overall: 'that our fight became accompanied by twin suffering.'
segments.s432-g55: تن ژنده پیل is a noun phrase 'the body (= creature) of ragged (ژنده) elephant' i.e. 'the ragged-bodied elephant' (ژنده is an adjectival descriptor). اندر آمد به خاک is an archaic/poetic verbal phrase 'came into the earth' = 'fell to the ground' (اندر آمد = entered/arrived into; به خاک = onto the ground).
segments.s432-g56: دل ما is 'our heart(s)'. ازین is a fused prepositional phrase 'from this' (از + این; here tokenized with ین representing این). درد is 'pain/sorrow' (emotional/afflictive sense), کردند is past plural 'they/made (it so)' used causatively/performatively, and چاک is a resultative noun 'gash/rent' — together 'they made our hearts rent from this pain' i.e. 'our hearts were torn by this pain.'
segments.s432-g81: همی گفت is a habitual/narrative past construction: همی (an adverbial marker of continuous or iterative action in epic style) + گفت (“said”). زار here qualifies the manner—'lamenting'—and ای یل اسفندیار is a vocative address (“O hero Esfandiyar”). Word order: manner (زار) and vocative follow the verb phrase for poetic emphasis.
segments.s432-g82: جهانجوی is a compound epithet meaning 'world-seeking' or ambitious, followed by و 'and'. از here means 'of/from' in the possessive/genitive sense and تخمه شهریار = 'the seed/offspring of the king' (تخمه = offspring, شهریار = king). The short phrase apposes Esfandiyar with his epithets.
segments.s432-g83: که کند introduces a rhetorical question/relative idea: که ('who/that') + کند (do/make). این‌چنین modifies کوه جنگی ('so/that kind of war-like mountain'); ز جای = 'from its place' (ز = poetic 'from', جای = place). The verb کند is present/narrative (not perfective) here used rhetorically.
segments.s432-g84: The second که introduces a relative clause describing that mountain: افگند is a past/preterit verb 'cast/caused to fall', شیر is 'lion' and ژیان is a qualifying noun ('life' in literal sense, here part of the epithet 'lion Zhiyān'), را marks the definite object, and ز پای is an idiom 'from the feet' = 'to the ground.'
segments.s432-g85: Again که کند forms a rhetorical 'who would make' clause. این پسندیده دندان پیل is a noun phrase: این ('this') + پسندیده ('chosen/prized') modifying دندان پیل ('elephant's tusk' — دندان used as 'tusk' and پیل = elephant). The adjective پسندیده is postpositive/attributive in poetic order.
segments.s432-g86: که here is a relative 'which/that'. آگند با = 'filled with' (آگند = filled/replete), موج دریای نیل = 'the waves of the Nile' (موج = wave, دریای = genitive 'sea/of the sea', نیل here taken as the Nile). The preposition/covering با links آگند to what it is filled with.
segments.s432-g87: چه آمد is an exclamatory question 'what has come/happened'. برین is بر + این (poetic clitic spelling) = 'upon this', تخمه = 'offspring', از چشم بد = 'from the evil eye' (چشم بد = evil eye). The clause asks what misfortune the child/offspring has suffered because of an evil gaze.
segments.s432-g88: که introduces the reason/explanation: بر بدکنش = 'upon the evildoer' (بر = upon, بدکنش = the evil-doer), بی‌گمان = 'undoubtedly', بد رسد = 'bad will befall' (رسد here = 'befall/happen to'). The whole clause gives the moral: surely the wrongdoer will suffer.
segments.s432-g113: همان is a demonstrative/emphatic determiner ('the same', 'that very') placed before the noun to single it out. پاک‌زاده is a compound adjective/noun 'noble‑born' that directly modifies نیاکان; نیاکان ما is a possessive construction with the possessive pronoun ما postposed ('our ancestors'). The phrase is a marked nominal phrase introducing the people being spoken of.
segments.s432-g114: گزیده functions as an adjectival/epithet meaning 'chosen' or 'the elite', followed by another epithet سرافراز ('honored/lofty'); و simply links to the following noun phrase پاکان ما ('the pure ones of ours'), where پاکان is a plural noun 'the pure' and ما is the possessive pronoun 'our'. Together the sequence is appositive to the preceding segment.
segments.s432-g115: برفتند is a 3rd‑person plural past verb 'they left/departed'. و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. ما را is the object pronoun with the explicit object marker را ('to us' / 'us' as the recipients). سپردند is the 3rd‑person plural past of سپردن 'to entrust/hand over'. جای here means 'place/position' and is the thing entrusted. Word order in poetry places the verb early, then the object phrase.
segments.s432-g116: نماند is used impersonally (past negative sense) meaning 'was not left' / 'did not remain'. کس here is 'anyone' but in this construction nماند کس = 'no one remained' (sense 'no one'). اندر is the locative preposition 'in/within'. سپنجی is an adjective 'spongy' (literal or metaphorical) qualifying سرای ('house/abode'); together they mean 'in the spongy house'.
segments.s432-g117: فراوان is an adverb 'frequently/greatly' modifying the verb that follows. بکوشیدم is 1st‑person singular past of کوشیدن 'I strove / I tried'. اندر جهان is a prepositional phrase 'in the world' (اندر = 'in', جهان = 'world') describing the scope of the effort.
segments.s432-g118: چه ... و چه ... is a correlative construction meaning 'whether ... or ...'. The first چه introduces the first alternative 'در آشکار' ('in the open/ openly'), where در is the preposition 'in' and آشکار functions adverbially 'openly'. و is 'and', the second چه introduces 'اندر نهان' ('in secret'), with اندر = 'in' and نهان = 'secretly'.
segments.s432-g119: که تا here forms a purpose clause 'so that / in order that'. رای یزدان is a genitive phrase with رای ('counsel/plan/decision') governed by یزدان ('God'). به جای آورم is an idiom 'to put into place / to accomplish'; آورم is 1st‑person singular subjunctive/optative ('may I bring about'), expressing purpose.
segments.s432-g120: خرد را makes 'wisdom' the definite/direct object (را marks the object). بدین رهنمای is an instrumental/locative phrase 'by/with this guidance' (بدین = 'by this' / 'with this', رهنمای = 'guide/guidance'). آورم is again 1st‑person subjunctive 'may I bring/bring about'; together the clause states the intended result of the earlier purpose clause.
segments.s432-g57: برفتد is a past narrative verb (classical style) meaning ‘went/set off’; here it governs the subject that follows. هر دو is the subject meaning ‘both (of them)’. پیاده is an adverbial/adjunct meaning ‘on foot’, and دوان is a participial/adverbial form ‘running/rushing’ that modifies the action — together ‘پیاده دوان’ = ‘running on foot’. Note the slightly inverted, poetic order: verb before subject.
segments.s432-g58: ز پیش سپه is a prepositional phrase: ز پیش = ‘from/in front of’ and سپه = ‘the host/army’. تا marks the extent/limit ‘as far as / up to’, and بر پهلوان = ‘toward/on the champion’ with بر indicating direction. The whole phrase specifies motion from the army up to the champion.
segments.s432-g59: بدیدند is simple past, 3rd-person plural ‘they saw’. جنگی here is adjectival ‘warlike/martial’ qualifying برش; برش is used as a noun meaning ‘a cut/wound’ (literary). پر ز خون is a postpositive phrase ‘full of blood’ (پر = ‘full (of)’, ز = ‘of’). The word order (noun + postpositive modifiers) is characteristic of poetic diction.
segments.s432-g60: یکی is an indefinite numeral ‘a certain / one’. تیر = ‘arrow’. پرخون is a compound adjective ‘bloodied / gory’ (postpositive). به here marks spatial relation ‘into / in’ (here ‘in (his) hand’), دست = ‘hand’, and اندرون = ‘inner part / inside’, so به دست اندرون = ‘in his inner hand’ or ‘in his hand inside’. The phrase describes a single bloody arrow held inwardly.
segments.s432-g61: پشوتن is the proper name (subject). بر و جامه را is a coordinated object phrase ‘(the) bar (cloak) and garment’ with را marking definiteness/object. کرد چاک is a light-verb construction: کرد ‘made/did’ plus the noun چاک ‘a tear/gash’ = ‘tore’. In epic diction objects precede the light verb.
segments.s432-g62: خروشان is an adverbial participle ‘roaring / in a tumultuous manner’. به سر = ‘to/at the head’ (i.e. his head). بر here functions as a directional/local preposition ‘on/upon’. همی + کرد is iterative/classical phrasing (‘he did repeatedly / he went on to do’), and خاک is the noun ‘dust/earth’ — کرد خاک = ‘threw/smeared dust’. Together: ‘roaring, he cast dust upon his head’ (a gesture of mourning).
segments.s432-g63: همی گشت is a classical iterative/perfective pattern meaning ‘kept turning / went about’. بهمن is a personal name (subject). به خاک اندرون is a locative phrase ‘into the earth / inward to the dust’ (به = into/toward, خاک = earth, اندرون = inside), indicating Bahman’s motion or posture.
segments.s432-g64: بمالید is past ‘he rubbed / he smeared’. رخ را is the definite object ‘his face’ (را marks object). بدان is the instrumental/prepositional sequence ‘with that’ (here split in the tokenization as ب + دان; functionally ‘with that’), گرم خون = ‘warm blood’ (گرم adjective + خون noun). The clause means ‘he rubbed his face with that warm blood.’
segments.s432-g41: Here به functions as an instrumental/agentive preposition 'by' (literary use) governing یک تیر 'a single arrow'. تیر is the head noun and برگشتی is a postpositive adjective 'returned/ricocheting' describing the arrow; از کارزار 'from the battle' specifies the arrow's origin with از.
segments.s432-g42: بخفتی is the 2nd-person past/formal narrative of خفتن used here meaning 'you fell' / 'you were struck (fell dead)' in an archaic/poetic sense. بران is a locative/adverbial 'upon that' and باره is a poetic noun meaning 'occasion/instance'; نامدار is a postpositive adjective 'renowned' modifying باره, so the phrase reads 'you fell upon that famed occasion.'
segments.s432-g43: هم‌اکنون 'right now' sets the time. به خاک اندر آید is a motion phrase: به (towards/into) + خاک (the earth) + اندر (in/into) and آید (comes) — literal 'comes into the earth' meaning 'falls to the ground'; سرت is 'your head' (with the 2nd-person possessive implicit). The verb آید is third-person used narratively to indicate an immediate/resulting action.
segments.s432-g44: بسوزد is a present/jussive-form of سوختن with the prefix ب-, used here to express an immediate result or fate ('will burn' / 'may burn') rather than a literal fire; دل مهربان مادرت is a genitive chain 'the heart of your kind mother' with the 2nd-person suffix ت attached to مادر ('your mother'). The clause therefore says her heart will (figuratively) burn with grief.
segments.s432-g45: هم‌انگه is an archaic/poetic temporal adverb 'at that moment.' سر نامبردار شاه is a nominal chain meaning 'the head of the renowned king': سر (head) followed by the adjective/epithet نامبردار ('renowned') and the head's possessor شاه ('king') in a compact poetic order.
segments.s432-g46: نگون is an adjective 'ill‑fated/dooméd' applied to the subject; اندر آمد is a verbal phrase 'came in / came forth' (اندر is a literary preposition 'in/into' and آمد 'came'). ز is the poetic preposition 'from' and پشت سیاه literally 'the dark/back' (سیاه here 'dark/gloomy'), so ز پشت سیاه describes the direction/source: 'from the dark back.'
segments.s432-g47: زمانی همی بود is a temporal phrase 'for a while he was / remained' (زمانی = 'for a time', همی = literary progressive/emphatic marker, بود = past 'was'). تا introduces the result/time clause 'until' and یافت هوش is the idiom 'found awareness' = 'regained consciousness', with یافت (found/obtained) used transitively on هوش (awareness).
segments.s432-g48: بر خاک بنشست is a simple action: بر (on/upon) + خاک (the ground) + بنشست (sat down) = 'sat upon the ground.' و is the coordinating 'and.' بگشاد is the prefixed form (ب-) of گشودن 'to open' used here as 'opened' and گوش 'ear' in this idiom means 'he pricked up his ears' or 'gave attention.' The b- prefix is common in literary Persian forms.
segments.s432-g65: پشوتن is a proper name (subject). همی before a past verb (گفت) is a classical/historical progressive/habitual marker meaning 'would/kept on' (not the modern 'still'). گفت is the past tense 'said/told' and راز جهان is the direct object ('the secret of the world') placed after the verb in this poetic word order.
segments.s432-g66: که introduces a relative clause qualifying راز جهان ('which'). داند is present-tense 'knows' (used here as a relative meaning 'knew' or 'was known by'). ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (source/agentive), and the coordinated noun phrase دین‌آوران و مهان ('the religion-bringers and the guests') is the phrase from whom/among whom the secret is known.
segments.s432-g67: چو here means 'as'/'like' (introducing a comparison). اسفندیاری is the proper name Esfandiyār. که begins a relative clause describing him. از بهر دین is a compound prepositional phrase 'for the sake of religion' (از + بهر = 'because of/for the sake of'; دین = 'religion').
segments.s432-g68: به (here 'toward/against') introduces the indirect object/target 'مردی' ('a man'). برآهیخت is a past, poetic perfect-like form meaning 'rose up' or 'lifted up' (here transitive: 'lifted/raised'), and شمشیر کین is a noun phrase 'the sword of vengeance' with the genitive attribute کین placed after شمشیر.
segments.s432-g69: جهان کرد پاک shows the verb-object-adjective order common in poetry: 'made the world pure' (کرد = 'made/did', پاک = 'pure'). از marks the source 'from', and بد بت‌پرست is the phrase 'evil idolater(s)' from which the world was cleansed.
segments.s432-g70: به بد کار is a prepositional phrase 'to/for evil deed(s)'; هرگز (never) negates the action that follows. نیازید دست is an archaic idiomatic sequence where نیازید (past form) means 'laid/put' or 'reached' (i.e., 'laid a hand to'); دست is the object 'hand'. Altogether: 'his hand never laid to evil work' = 'he never engaged in evil deeds.'
segments.s432-g71: به‌روز جوانی is a temporal phrase 'in the day/prime of youth' (به‌روز = 'in the day/of the day' used poetically). هلاک آمدش is an idiomatic patter 'ruin/destruction came upon him' where آمدش = 'came to him' (آمد + enclitic -ش = 'to him').
segments.s432-g72: سر تاجور is a noun phrase 'the head of the tajur/merchant' (no linking particle in poetry). سوی خاک آمدش is literal 'came toward the earth' (سوی = 'toward', خاک = 'earth/dust'), with آمدش again 'came to him/it' used to indicate falling/coming down.
segments.s432-g89: کجا شد is an interrogative + past verb ('where has ... become/where is ... gone'). به رزم functions as a locative/arena phrase ('in/for battle') with به taking the sense 'towards/into'. اندرون (inside) modifies ساز (here meaning a musical tune/instrument) and the possessive تو follows the noun phrase, attaching to the whole phrase (your war-music).
segments.s432-g90: Same structure: کجا شد introduces the question. به بزم here means 'at/in the bāzam (feast/assembly)'. آن خوش‌آواز تو is a NP with آن 'that/the', خوش‌آواز 'melodious' describing voice/song, and تو as the postnominal possessive referring back to the singer/owner.
segments.s432-g91: کجا شد is again the interrogative past. دل و هوش و آیین is a coordinated list of nouns (heart, intellect/wit, conduct/custom); the final تو is a postnominal possessive that applies to the whole coordinated phrase. آیین here reads as 'custom/conduct' rather than a ritual meaning.
segments.s432-g92: This continues the catalogue of personal attributes (توانایی و اختر و دین) coordinated and again closed by the possessive تو; there is no new verb, so these items are understood as also questioned by the preceding کجا شد. The nouns are listed in the normal Persian order and linked by و.
segments.s432-g93: چو functions as a temporal 'when'. کردی is the 2nd-person past 'you did/made' and governs جهان را (the world, with را marking it as definite object). ز + بدخواه means 'of/from ill-wishers'; پاک is a predicate meaning 'pure/clear' placed after the phrase — together: 'when you made the world free of ill-wishers.'
segments.s432-g94: نیامدت is negative past with the enclitic -ت ('did not come to you'); the clause says that 'no fear came to you from the elephant and the lion.' از marks source ('from'), پیل 'elephant' and شیر 'lion' are the sources, and باک is the noun 'fear'. The token وز in this classical/poetic sequence functions as 'and' (classical conjunction), here treated as irregular relative to the provided sense list.
segments.s432-g95: کنون 'now' sets the time. آمدت is the past verb 'came to you' (آمد + enclitic -ت). سودمندی is the thing that has arrived ('benefit/advantage') and به کار is a locative/benefactive phrase 'to/for the matter/affairs' — literally 'a benefit has come to your affairs.'
segments.s432-g96: که introduces the subordinate clause ('that/so that'). در خاک is locative 'in the dust/earth'. بیند is the 3rd-person present form of 'to see' (here jussive/hortatory sense: 'may see' or 'will see' in poetic register). ترا is the 2nd-person object (enclitic written separately here), and را is the object marker; روزگار ('time/fate') is the subject placed after the verb (a common classical inversion): 'that fate may see you in the dust.'
segments.s432-g9: همی before a verb is a classical/perfective-continuous particle here marking an ongoing or habitual perception; بینی is 2nd-person singular present ('you see' / 'you perceive'). The noun phrase این پاک جان مرا is the direct object: این 'this' + پاک 'pure' qualifying جان 'soul', followed by the patient/possessive sequence م…را (here the author wrote م + را) indicating 'of mine' (possessive suffix) plus the object marker را.
segments.s432-g10: This is a paratactic continuation: توان مرا 'my power/strength' and هم روان مرا 'and my spirit' are coordinated objects. توان is the noun 'power/ability' rather than the auxiliary; م…را again encodes the first‑person possessive + object marker (written here split), and هم before روان functions as 'also/too' linking the items.
segments.s432-g11: که at the start here has a purposive/optative sense ('so that' / 'in order that'). چندین is an adverb/adjective 'many / several' (here 'many times'), and بپیچم is a present/subjunctive form 'I may turn/ I may twist' (the prefix بـ and final -م mark 1st‑person). The following که introduces the clause which states the hoped-for result (Esfandiyar...).
segments.s432-g12: مگر here means 'unless/except' or 'so that (perhaps)' functioning to mark the contingency/result ('so that Esfandiyar might...'). سر بپیچاند is a light verb phrase 'turn his head/avert his head' (بپیچاند = 3rd‑person subjunctive/optative 'may turn'), and از کارزار literally 'from the battle' — از is the preposition 'from' and کارزار 'battle'.
segments.s432-g13: تو دانی is present tense 'you know' (دانی = you know). که introduces the content clause 'that...'. بیداد کوشد همی is a small clause where بیداد 'injustice/oppression' is the subject and کوشد (3rd‑person present/subjunctive/optative form of کوشیدن) means 'strives/works', with همی again giving the sense of continual action ('ever/continually').
segments.s432-g14: This continues the description of what 'injustice' does: همی at the start reiterates continual action; جنگ و مردی is a coordinated noun phrase 'war and manliness/valor' (مردی here means the quality 'manliness' rather than a single man); فروشد is the verb form meaning 'sells' (classical 3rd‑person), and the final همی again emphasizes repetition or constancy.
segments.s432-g15: This is an imperative negative: مگیر 'do not take / do not hold' is a 2nd‑person negative imperative. به بادافره is a prepositional phrase functioning adverbially 'as wind‑borne / as fleeting'; این گناهم is the direct object 'this sin of mine' (گناهم = گناه + possessive suffix -م 'my'). The construction asks the addressee not to regard the speaker's sin in that (fleeting) way.
segments.s432-g16: This is a vocative phrase addressing the addressee: توی here = 'in/among' used idiomatically with آفریننده 'Creator' as a title; ماه و تیر are two objects of the epithet (literally 'the moon and the arrow') used to invoke the Creator's power/roles. The whole phrase names/addresses God as 'Creator of the moon and the arrow.'
segments.s432-g145: سوی چاره گشتم ز بیچارگی = literal order: directional phrase + verb + cause. سوی (preposition) introduces the direction/goal 'toward'; چاره is the noun 'remedy/relief' and is the semantic goal. گشتم is past 1st‑person (گشتن used here as 'turn/turn toward') with the subject omitted (I). ز بیچارگی is a causal/ablative phrase 'because of/from misery' modifying the action.
segments.s432-g146: بدادم بدو سر به یکبارگی = archaic clause in which the verb 'gave/yielded' is expressed as بدادم (an older/coalesced past form of دادن); the implied subject is 'I'. بدو is the clitic/pronoun 'to him/unto him' marking the recipient. سر 'head' is the direct object; به یکبارگی is a locative/adverbial phrase 'in one sudden motion / at once'. Word order places object and clitic before the verb, a common poetic ordering.
segments.s432-g147: زمان ورا در کمان ساختم = زمان 'the moment/occasion' is the noun indicating timing; ورا is the enclitic pronoun 'it' (to/at it) attached after the noun. در کمان is the locative 'in/into the bow' and ساختم is first‑person past 'I made/placed' (ساختن) governing the whole clause. The object (ورا) and locative precede the verb, as is common in classical Persian poetic syntax.
segments.s432-g148: چو روزش سرآمد بینداختم = چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; روزش 'his/its day' (روزش = روز + possessive -ش). سرآمد functions as 'end/conclusion' ('when its day was over' — here used as the temporal head), and بینداختم is the first‑person past 'I cast/threw (it)' (بی‑دَاختن = to fling/let fly). The direct object is understood from the previous clause.
segments.s432-g149: گر او را همی روز باز آمدی = گر is the conditional 'if'. او را has را marking 'او' as the affected/ topical person; همی here is an emphatic/temporal particle ('that very' / 'just then') qualifying روز 'day', and باز means 'again'. آمدی is a past tense form 'came' (classical usage allows shifts in person/ mood for hypothetical/optative shades). The clause reads 'if he had come back on that very day'.
segments.s432-g150: مرا کار گز کی فراز آمدی = this line is compressed/elliptical: مرا 'me' (with object marker split as 'م' + 'را' in the edition), کار 'affair/ordeal' and گز likely names a prick/thorn or tamarisk used metaphorically for suffering; کی 'when' (or 'how/ever') and فراز آمدی literally 'you came from above / rose up'. Grammatically the clause strings together object (مرا) + possessed affair (کار گز) and then poses a temporal/interrogative (کی) with آمدی. The syntax is archaic and somewhat elliptical; the segment effectively asks when or how the speaker was brought to that plight or when 'you' (addressee/agent) came from on high.
segments.s432-g151: ازین خاک تیره بباید شدن = ازین ('از' + demonstrative 'این') = 'from this'; خاک تیره 'dark earth' is the object/source. بباید (impersonal/modal) = 'it is necessary / one must' and شدن (infinitive) completes the periphrastic construction meaning 'to get away / to become (gone)'. Together: 'one must get away from this dark earth.'
segments.s432-g152: به پرهیز یک دم نشاید زدن = به پرهیز 'in flight/avoidance' (به + noun) sets the manner; یک دم 'a single moment' is the time expression. نشاید is impersonal negative 'it is not fitting/allowed'; زدن here forms the idiom دم زدن ('to breathe / to pause / to take a moment') so نشاید زدن = 'one must not pause even a moment.' The clause therefore forbids any halting during escape. (Note: زدن participates in the compound idiom here rather than its literal 'strike' sense.)
segments.s432-g121: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when' (archaic/poetic). از من گرفت is phrased like a passive/experiential clause 'was taken from me' (گرفت used with passive sense), with the agent omitted; ای سخن روشنی is a vocative/appositive phrase 'O clear/brilliant speech' placed after the verb for poetic inversion.
segments.s432-g122: ز بد marks the cause ('because of/through evil'); بسته شد is a passive-past construction 'was closed' (شد as auxiliary/voice marker); راه آهرمنی is the affected noun phrase 'the way/path of Ahriman (the demonic one)'. Word order is inverted for poetic effect (verb before subject).
segments.s432-g123: زمانه is the subject meaning 'time/the world/fortune'; بیازید is a poetic past/precative verb 'gaped/opened' (here: 'bared' or 'unsheathed'); چنگال تیز is the object/complement 'sharp talons' placed after the verb in noun + adjective order (typical Persian adjective follows its noun).
segments.s432-g124: نبد is an archaic/contractional negative 'نبود' = 'there was not' (negating existence); زو = از او ('from it') indicates source; مرا marks 'for me' (dative/accusative); روزگار گریز is a compound-like phrase 'escape from fate/fortune' (روزگار = fate, گریز = escape). Taken together: 'There was no escape for me from it.'
segments.s432-g125: امید من 'my hope' (possessive construction; من is genitive in context). آنست = آن است 'it is that' (copular construction introducing a clause). کاندر is an archaic fused form of 'که + اندر' meaning 'in which' or 'that in'; بهشت = 'paradise'. The clause-head sets up a following dependent clause describing the hope.
segments.s432-g126: دل‌افروز من modifies the earlier 'بهشت' as 'my heart-delighting / delightful to me'. بدرود here is used idiomatically (poetic) with the sense of loss/perishing rather than the modern interjection 'farewell' (the exact lexeme is archaic); هرچ = 'whatever'; کشت in the agricultural sense 'that which is sown / cultivated'. The clause expresses the speaker's wish about the fate of what he has sown in Paradise.
segments.s432-g127: به مردی is a prepositional phrase 'to/for a man' or 'as for manliness' (contextually 'not by a mere man'); مرا here is object 'me' (the victim); پور دستان is a name-construction 'the son/descendant of Dastān' (پور as 'son of'); نکشت is the negative past 'did not kill' (here used transitively). The segment denies that the speaker was killed by a (mere) man or by that named person — note that پور دستان functions as a name, so word-order is emphatic.
segments.s432-g128: نگه کن is an imperative 'look/observe' (نگه = 'look/watch', کن = imperative 'do'); بدین گز is 'by/with this gaz' (بدین = 'by this/with this', گز = a unit of length, 'cubit/yard'); که دارم به مشت is a relative clause 'which I hold in my fist' (دارم = 'I have/hold', به مشت = 'in the fist'). The whole segment is an imperative invitation to note the measure/tools the speaker holds.
segments.s432-g17: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when' introducing a time clause. خودکامه and جنگی form a compound description 'self-willed (خودکامه) fighter/warrior (جنگی)'; بدید is the simple past 'saw'; آن درنگ = 'that delay' (آن pointing back to the pause/delay). The word order is typical of classical Persian: subordinate time phrase (چو + subject + verb) followed by the object phrase (آن درنگ).
segments.s432-g18: که begins a subordinate clause 'that/so that' linking the previous time clause to the result. همی before the adjective/verb (همی دیر شد) is an archaic/epic particle giving emphasis or progressive force; here it reinforces the past action. دیر شد is a two-word verbal idea: دیر (late) + شد (became) = 'became late' (past). سوی جنگ is a prepositional phrase 'toward the battle' showing direction.
segments.s432-g19: بدو is the prepositional clitic 'to him' (he said to him). گفت is the past tense verb 'said' (the provided sense list did not include the verbal reading, so the sense index is set to -1). کای is the vocative 'O', used to address someone; سگزی بدگمان is an insult: سگزی (base/doglike) + بدگمان ('suspicious' / 'distrustful') as descriptive epithets.
segments.s432-g20: نشد is the negative past of 'become' (here functioning as a negative result/wish — a curse-like optative: 'may ... not become/satisfy'). سیر جانت = 'the satiety/fulfilment of your soul/life' (سیر as noun 'satiety', جانت 'your soul' as term of address). ز تیر و کمان = 'from/by arrow and bow' (ز = from/of). Together it expresses 'may your soul not be satiated by arrow and bow'.
segments.s432-g21: ببینی is the classical/persian present-subjunctive or jussive form (b- prefix + بین stem + -ی) used here as an exhortation/narrative 'behold' or 'you see'. کنون = 'now'. تیر گشتاسپی is a noun-noun genitive juxtaposition 'the arrow of Gushtasp' (no izafet marker in epic diction).
segments.s432-g22: دل شیر is an adjectival/nominal phrase literally 'heart (دل) of a lion (شیر)' → 'lion‑hearted' (brave). و links the two items; پیکان لهراسپی = 'the arrows of Lohrasp' (پیکان + name لهراسپی in genitive juxtaposition). The order places the qualities and possessions after the main noun for poetic effect.
segments.s432-g23: یکی تیر = 'one (a certain) arrow' (یکی here numeral/indefinite). بر is the preposition 'on/upon/against' and ترگ رستم is 'Rostam's garment/cloth' by juxtaposition (ترگ used as a piece of cloth/rag). بزد is the past tense 'struck/ hit' (3rd person past). The sentence follows SOV tendencies with prepositional phrase before verb.
segments.s432-g24: چنان = 'so/thus' introducing a result or degree: 'so much so that...'. کز is the archaic contracted form of 'که از / که ز' meaning 'that/from which' linking to the consequence. کمان سواران = 'the horsemen's bows' (کمان + سواران in genitive relation), and سزد is the impersonal/present form 'it befits/it is fitting'. The clause as a whole comments on the degree/effect of the previous blow: 'so that it befits the riders' bows.'
segments.s432-g73: بدی را: 'بدی' is a definite noun ('evil, harm') marked by the object/ topicalizing marker را, which here highlights the particular calamity. کزو is a fused relative/adverb meaning 'from which / from that', linking that evil to the following clause. هست is the copula 'is/there is' and گیتی ('the world') is the grammatical subject; به درد is a prepositional phrase 'in/with pain' describing the state's effect — literally 'there is (such) evil from which the world is in pain.'
segments.s432-g74: پرآزار is an adjective 'very harmful / grievous' qualifying the kind of pain. ازو is a prepositional pronoun 'from him/it' (pointing back to the cause). جان here means 'life' or 'soul', and آزاد مرد is a noun phrase 'the free/noble man' (i.e. the valiant man); the whole phrase states that the noble man's life departed as a result — note classical Persian often places the affected noun phrase without an explicit verb like 'departed' nearby, so this segment completes the previous clause.
segments.s432-g75: فراوان is an adverb/adjectival intensifier 'abundantly / many', modifying the verbal phrase. برو بگذرد is a periphrastic expression ('go and pass' → 'pass away, elapse'); here it conveys the idea of time passing. روزگار is the noun 'time / days / fortunes' and functions as the thing that 'passes'. Word order places the adverb before the verbal compound, which is typical in classical Persian.
segments.s432-g76: که introduces a subordinate clause giving the wished-for result/reason: 'so that / that'. هرگز نبیند is negative-subjunctive/hoped-for meaning 'may he never see' (هرگز = 'never', نبیند = subjunctive/negative form of 'to see'). بد کارزار is a nominal phrase 'a bad/unfavorable battlefield' (بد qualifying کارزار); the clause expresses the wish that he never have to witness such a dire fight.
segments.s432-g77: جوانان is the plural subject 'the young men / youths' (noun). گرفتندش is the past tense verb 'they seized him' (گرفتند + enclitic object -ش). اندر کنار is a locative/adverbial phrase 'to the side / aside / in company', indicating where they took him; classical word order places the verb before the locative phrase without any auxiliary.
segments.s432-g78: همی is an emphatic/continuative adverb (here indicating the action that follows). خون is 'blood'. ستردند is a poetic/past-tense verb here meaning they caused or took blood (an archaic/poetic sense close to 'drew / removed / inflicted bloodshed'); the exact classical verb sense is idiomatic. زان is the prepositional/relative 'from that / of that' pointing to the previously mentioned person, and شهریار is 'the king'. Together the clause says they (then) caused bloodshed in respect of that king.
segments.s432-g79: پشوتن is a proper name (the lamenting person). بروبر is an adverbial phrase 'face-to-face / directly' modifying the verb. همی here indicates continuous or habitual action ('kept on / continually'). مویه کرد is a light-verb construction: مویه (lament) as the noun + کرد ('did/made') to form 'he lamented / bewailed'. The sequence follows classical Persian pattern noun + light verb.
segments.s432-g80: رخی is 'his/one's face' (classical nominal form of رخ). پر ز خون is a fixed adjectival phrase 'full of blood' (پر = 'full (of)', ز = 'of/from', خون = 'blood'). و links the two parallel predicates; دلی here denotes 'heart' (emotive center) and پر ز درد parallels the previous construction: 'full of pain/sorrow'. The whole segment gives two vivid physical/emotional images: a bloodied face and a heart full of suffering.
segments.s432-g97: که here begins an optative/exclamatory clause (‘that’/‘may it be that’); نفرین is the direct object meaning “a curse”; بر is the preposition “on/upon” and combines with the following demonstrative (برین = بر + این) to mean “upon this”; تاج ‘crown’ and این تخت ‘this throne’ are the two things cursed; باد is the optative verb ‘may it be’ (classical Persian optative). Together the clause literally says “that a curse upon this crown and this throne may be.”
segments.s432-g98: بدین کوشش بیش = بدین (به + این) “by/for this”; here the tokenizer split بدین into ب + دین but the second piece in the list does not match the actual element ‘این’ (see senses). کوشش ‘effort’ is the noun; بیش ‘more/greater’ follows to mean ‘greater effort’; و links the two parallel wishes; این بخت ‘this fortune’ (بخت = fortune/fate); باد again is the optative ‘may it be’. The whole clause is a parallel optative: “may there be greater effort for this and greater fortune.”
segments.s432-g99: که introduces the subordinate clause (here giving the reason/condition); چو تو = ‘when/as (there is) you’ or more idiomatically ‘when there is one like you’; چو in classical verse often means ‘when’ or ‘as’; تو is the 2nd‑singular pronoun; سواری ‘rider/horseman’ is the head noun modified by the following adjectives دلیر و جوان (‘brave and young’). Word order is typical Persian: modifier adjectives follow the noun cluster introduced by a simile marker چو.
segments.s432-g100: سرافراز ‘proud / honored / lofty’ and دانا ‘wise’ and روشن‑روان (a compound adjective meaning something like ‘bright‑soul’d’ or ‘clear‑minded’). The adjectives are simply coordinated with و; they further describe the rider (سواری) introduced in the previous segment. This string of epithets is typical epic epithet stacking after the noun.
segments.s432-g101: بدین سان = ‘in this manner / thus’ (here written as ب + دین + سان in the segmentation; the middle piece in the provided list does not correspond to the demonstrative این and is therefore flagged below); شود is the present/future‑oriented classical auxiliary ‘become / will be’ and marks a passive result; کشته is the past‑participle ‘killed/slain’ functioning as predicate nominal; در کارزار ‘in battle’ is the locative prepositional phrase. Together: “Thus he will be slain in battle.”
segments.s432-g102: به زاری ‘with lamentation / in mourning’ (به marks manner or cause); سرآید is the verb here meaning ‘come/arrive’ or, idiomatically, ‘come to an end’ (classical use allows ‘to end’); برو روزگار is a terse poetic ordering meaning ‘(his) days/time (will) pass/are gone’ — روزگار here = fate/days. The clause as a whole is idiomatic: “his days/fate will end in lamentation.”
segments.s432-g103: که again introduces an optative clause; مه here is an archaic/classical form (used in Ferdowsi) functioning like a first‑person possessive/emphatic ‘my’ or ‘may it be to me’ (the provided sense list does not include this classical pronoun, so it is flagged below); تاج ‘crown’; بادا is the optative verb ‘may it be’; و links the parallel wish; the second مه likewise marks possession or the speaker’s desire regarding تخت شاه ‘the king’s throne’. In short, two parallel optatives: “may the crown be mine and may the king’s throne be mine.”
segments.s432-g104: This segment lists proper names and a noun in apposition or as the objects/possessors linked to the previous wish: the repeated مه again functions as the archaic possessive/optative marker (not represented in the provided senses, flagged below); گشتاسپ and جاماسپ are the proper names Gushtasp and Jamasp joined by و; آن بارگاه ‘that palace / that royal court’ completes the series. Grammatically these are coordinated nouns following the preceding clause — a poetic string of owners/locations tied to the wish.
segments.s432-g105: چنین گفت is a fixed formula meaning “thus spoke/said.” The epithet پر دانش ('full of knowledge') precedes the name اسفندیار as a poetic title: literally ‘thus said, full-of-knowledge Esfandiyar.’ Note that گفت here is the reporting verb; the form and order are archaic/poetic.
segments.s432-g106: که‌ای is an exclamatory/vocative particle here (older form of 'ای' or 'که ای') addressing someone: “O…”. مرد دانای به روزگار = “man, wise in the time/age”; دانای is an adjective/noun ‘wise’, and به روزگار is a prepositional phrase ‘in/of the age’ qualifying the addressee.
segments.s432-g107: مکن is the classical negative imperative 'do not.' خویشتن is a reflexive/emphatic object 'yourself.' پیش من = 'in my presence/before me.' بر تباه is a predicative phrase 'in a ruined/corrupt state' placed after the object; together: 'Do not (show) yourself before me ruined.'
segments.s432-g108: چنین بود is a simple past copula ‘it was thus.’ بهر من = 'for me' (dative/benefactive), and از تاج و گاه links the reason/field being discussed 'concerning the crown and (the) seat/throne.' Note گاه here is poetic; the clause states how things stood for the speaker with regard to rulership.
segments.s432-g109: تن کشته را = 'the body of the slain' with را marking the (definite/emphatic) object. خاک باشد is a predicative construction 'become/let be dust/earth' (باشد used optatively/coprial), and نهال at the end is the vocative/noun continued into the next segment ('sapling' = metaphor for a young man).
segments.s432-g110: تو = 'you.' از کشتن من = ablative/instrumental phrase 'by/through killing me.' بدین سان is the adverbial phrase 'in this manner/so' (here split orthographically into ب + دین + سان in the data), and منال is the noun 'attainment/desire/goal' — 'do not consider such a gain by killing me.' Word order places the ablative and manner before the final object (منال).
segments.s432-g111: کجا شد is an interrogative phrase 'where did/has ... become/where are ... gone?' followed by the list of names (فریدون و هوشنگ و جم). Classical/poetic usage uses شد after کجا to ask about whereabouts or fate.
segments.s432-g112: ز باد آمده = a participial/ablative phrase 'having come from/on the wind' (ز = from, آمده = come (participle)). باز گردد is a jussive/subjunctive sense 'may/should return' (باز = again), and به دم = 'in a breath/moment' (دم as a brief moment). The clause sets a conditional/hypothetical sense: if they came by the wind, they could return at once.
segments.s432-g33: گرفته is a past participle (from گرفتن) meaning 'taken/covered/affected'. The following enclitic بش here functions like an object/third‑person clitic ('to him'/'upon him') so the phrase reads roughly 'was taken/was covered on him'. The clause then continues with یالِ اسپِ سیاه (the black steed's mane) as a coordinated noun phrase: '... and the black steed's mane'.
segments.s432-g34: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. لعل شد is a verbal phrase where لعل (ruby/deep red) is the result and شد is the past 'became' — 'became ruby'. خاک آوردگاه (the soil/earth of the battlefield) is the subject that undergoes that change: 'the battlefield's earth became ruby from blood.' Word order is the common poetic inversion (result + شد after the source phrase).
segments.s432-g35: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so'. گفت is the simple past 'said' (here used in poetry with the subject after the verb); رستم is the subject (the speaker). به اسفندیار is the indirect object 'to Esfandiyar' (preposition + name).
segments.s432-g36: که introduces the content/relative clause 'that/which'. آوردی is 2nd‑person past 'you brought/caused'. آن تخم زفتی is a noun phrase: آن 'that' + تخم 'seed/offspring' used contemptuously + زفتی an insulting adjective 'rotten/nasty'. به بار here is part of the idiom به بار آوردن 'to bring to bearing/produce', so the whole clause says 'that you have brought that nasty seed into being.'
segments.s432-g37: تو 'you' + آنی here functions as a demonstrative/pronoun ('the one'/'you are he who') and that is followed by a relative clause introduced by که 'who/that'. گفتی is 2nd‑person past 'you said' and the second که introduces the reported content. رویین تنم is a noun phrase 'my iron‑clad body' (رویین = iron‑hard/armored, تن‑م = 'my body' with possessive suffix).
segments.s432-g38: بلند is an adverb/adjective 'high/lofty' modifying the following phrase. آسمان with بلند yields 'to the high sky' and the first بر is directional 'toward/to'. زمین is 'earth/ground' and the second بر combined with زنم (from زدن) yields 'to strike upon'; زنم is 1st‑person singular (present/subjunctive form used here to express a vowed or future action) literally 'I strike' or idiomatically 'I will strike'. The clause thus says 'I would/shall rise toward the high heaven and strike upon the earth.'
segments.s432-g39: من is the subject 'I'. از is the ablative preposition 'from'. شست تو here means 'your hand/thumb' (in older usage شست can refer to the hand/fist as the source of force) indicating source/agent of the blows. هشت تیر خدنگ is the object phrase 'eight (هشت) arrows (تیر) of the type خدنگ' (خدنگ = dagger/short‑sword; here a kind of lethal arrow or wound). Word order is numeral + noun + modifier, typical in Persian.
segments.s432-g40: بخوردم is past 'I received/sustained' (here 'I was struck / I took'); ننالیدم is negated past 'I did not moan/complain'. از introduces the domain 'about/concerning' (از نام و ننگ = 'about fame and disgrace'); نام here means 'reputation/fame' and ننگ 'shame/disgrace'. The clause therefore states 'I took X blows; I did not lament about fame or shame.'
